does: This paper investigates whether causal intervention techniques used in mechanistic interpretability—such as targeted activation manipulations to probe what neural network representations encode—push internal representations away from their natural, in-distribution state. The authors develop theoretical and empirical frameworks distinguishing between two types of representational divergence: harmless divergences occurring within the behavioral null-space that don't affect model outputs, and pernicious divergences that activate unused network pathways and induce spurious behavioral changes. To address the problematic cases, they adapt and apply the Counterfactual Latent loss to constrain interventional representations closer to the natural distribution, improving the fidelity of mechanistic explanations derived from interventions.
connects: This work builds on the mechanistic interpretability literature that uses causal interventions—including activation patching, counterfactual analysis, and targeted perturbations—to understand learned representations in neural networks. It extends prior efforts to validate these interpretability techniques by examining whether their explanations reflect genuine model properties or artifacts of the intervention process itself. The paper engages with broader concerns about out-of-distribution behavior in neural networks and connects to work on neural network alignment and representation learning that examines when internal states remain faithful across different computational regimes. The application of Counterfactual Latent losses draws on recent advances in controlled representation learning and distribution-aware interventions.
enables: This work opens pathways for developing more trustworthy mechanistic interpretability methods by establishing criteria for identifying when interventions produce faithful versus misleading insights. It suggests future research into intervention protocols that preserve distributional properties while still enabling causal probing of learned functions. The theoretical framework distinguishing harmless and pernicious divergences provides tools for practitioners to assess mechanistic explanations critically, potentially leading to safer deployment of interpretability techniques in high-stakes domains. Broader implications include improved methods for neural network auditing, safer extraction of learned concepts for knowledge transfer, and more robust verification of what neural networks actually compute under natural operating conditions.
